slides.title: The Primitive Problem 🧱
slides.content:
In Chapter 5, we learned about `ArrayList`, a dynamic list that can grow and shrink.

But there is a catch: The entire Java Collections Framework (ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet) is built to hold **Objects**.

It absolutely REFUSES to hold primitive data types like `int`, `double`, or `boolean`.

slides.title: The Compiler Error ❌
slides.content: If you try to make an ArrayList of `int`, Java will throw a compiler error complaining about unexpected types.

slides.title: The Wrapper Solution 🎁
slides.content:
To solve this, Java created **Wrapper Classes**.

These are fully-fledged Object Classes whose entire job is to hold a single primitive value inside them, wrapping it up like a gift so that Collections will accept it.

slides.title: Auto-Boxing 📦
slides.content:
Instead of `int`, use `Integer`.
Instead of `double`, use `Double`.
Instead of `boolean`, use `Boolean`.

Java is smart enough to "Auto-box" your raw primitive numbers into the Wrapper automatically.

quizData.question: Which of the following describes why we use `ArrayList<Integer>` instead of `ArrayList<int>`?

options.text: Because Integer takes up less memory.
Because Java Collections are designed to ONLY store Objects, and `int` is a primitive. `Integer` is the Object wrapper for `int`.
It is just a stylistic choice.

quizData.explanation: Primitives are raw data. Collections demand complex Objects.
